_about: HEURISTIC, hand-set coarse priors for rule+frequency candidate scoring.
These are NOT corpus frequencies -- this repo ships no corpus. 'Frequency' here
means coarse, documented POS/source plausibility priors, not learned Zipf counts.
Every number is a rule weight, kept in DATA (not hardcoded in Python) per the
project contract. The Python holds only the generic matching/scoring machinery.
Extension point: score_candidate() will read an optional per-lemma 'frequency'
field off the lexicon (frequency_weight below) once such counts exist; until then
no lexicon entry carries it, so that term is a documented no-op.
All scores feed a bounded, sum-normalized HEURISTIC confidence that ONLY the
sentence layer sets -- never a learned probability, never on word-level analyze().

context_rules.name: gen_before_poss
context_rules.doc: R1: a token after a genitive-marked word prefers a possessive reading (onun evi -> ev+poss3sg; also noun genitives kitabin kapisi).

context_rules.name: num_before_noun
context_rules.doc: R3: a numeral immediately before a noun prefers the bare-nominal NUM reading (Yuz kisi -> yuz=NUM).

context_rules.name: acc_before_verb
context_rules.doc: R2: a content word before a finite verb, NOT preceded by a genitive, prefers the accusative direct-object reading (evi gordum -> ev+accusative). Guarded off after a genitive because a possessed object would surface with -ni (evini), so the genitive-left evidence (R1) must win.

context_rules.name: final_finite_verb
context_rules.doc: R4: the last analyzable token of a sentence prefers a finite-verb reading (reinforces yuzuyor / birak-tik as the predicate over a nominal/participle homograph).

context_rules.name: adj_before_noun
context_rules.doc: R5: a word immediately before a noun prefers an adjective reading (hasta cocuk -> hasta=ADJ). With the current lexicon this reinforces (the analyzer already ranks ADJ before NOUN for an equal-length homograph) and raises the chosen candidate's confidence; it will flip the pick once a NOUN-primary homograph enters the lexicon.
